questions.stem: A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute-onset chest pain that began 6 hours ago. He reports that the pain is sharp, worsens when he takes a deep breath or lies flat, and improves when he sits up and leans forward. He had an upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. His temperature is 100.8°F. On auscultation, a high-pitched scratchy sound is heard over the left sternal border that varies with respiration. Which of the following anatomic structures is most directly responsible for the pain-relieving effect of leaning forward?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The visceral layer of the serous pericardium, which contains pain-sensing nerve fibers

choices.label: B
choices.text: The parietal layer of the serous pericardium, which is responsible for sensing pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fibrous pericardium, which is the outermost protective layer surrounding the heart

choices.label: D
choices.text: The epicardial adipose tissue, which transmits nociceptive signals to the central cortex

choices.label: E
choices.text: The myocardium itself, which becomes ischemic from the surrounding pericardial inflammation

explanation.summary: The chest pain in acute pericarditis improves with sitting up and leaning forward because this position reduces pressure on the parietal layer of the serous pericardium, which is the pain-sensitive layer.
explanation.whyCorrect: The parietal layer of the serous pericardium is innervated by somatic pain fibers and is responsible for sensing pain in pericarditis. Sitting up and leaning forward reduces the contact and pressure between the inflamed pericardial layers, specifically reducing stretch on the parietal pericardium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The visceral layer of the serous pericardium (epicardium) is not primarily responsible for pain sensation in acute pericarditis. Pain fibers are predominantly in the parietal layer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The fibrous pericardium is the outermost structural layer providing protection, but it is not the primary structure responsible for pain sensation in acute pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epicardial adipose tissue is a structural layer beneath the visceral pericardium and does not contain significant nociceptive nerve fibers responsible for pericardial pain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The myocardium is not directly responsible for the chest pain of pericarditis. The pain arises from inflammation of the pericardial layers, not from myocardial ischemia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with end-stage kidney disease on hemodialysis presents with progressive chest pain over the past 3 days. She admits to missing her last three dialysis sessions. Her blood urea nitrogen is 120 mg/dL and creatinine is 12.8 mg/dL. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation. An echocardiogram reveals a small pericardial effusion. A pericardial biopsy would most likely show which of the following pathologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thin, watery, straw-colored fluid with minimal inflammatory cells in the pericardial space

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dense neutrophil-rich inflammatory infiltrate with bacterial colonies within the pericardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemorrhagic fluid containing malignant cells with nuclear pleomorphism and mitotic figures

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrin deposition on the pericardial surfaces with associated acute inflammatory changes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dense fibrotic thickening of the pericardium with calcification and lymphocytic infiltration

explanation.summary: Uremia from inadequate dialysis is a classic cause of acute fibrinous pericarditis, which is characterized by deposition of fibrin on the pericardial surfaces. It is the most common pathologic type of acute pericarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uremia (from end-stage kidney disease with missed dialysis) is a well-established cause of fibrinous pericarditis. This type shows characteristic fibrin deposition on the pericardial surfaces, giving a 'bread and butter' gross appearance. It is the most common pathologic type of acute pericarditis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes serous pericarditis, which is characterized by thin, watery, straw-colored fluid with minimal inflammation. Serous pericarditis is typically caused by viral infections or autoimmune diseases, not uremia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This describes purulent pericarditis, caused by bacterial infections (most commonly Staphylococcus aureus). Uremia causes fibrinous, not purulent, pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This describes hemorrhagic pericarditis, which is most commonly caused by malignancies metastatic to the pericardium or invasive cardiac procedures, not uremia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This describes constrictive pericarditis, a chronic complication of pericarditis with fibrosis and calcification. This is not the acute pathologic finding expected in uremic pericarditis.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents with chest pain and fever 3 weeks after an ST-elevation myocardial infarction. The pain is pleuritic and improves when he leans forward. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation in leads not corresponding to his prior infarct territory. Echocardiogram shows a small pericardial effusion. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reinfarction of the same coronary territory causing recurrent ST-elevation and chest pain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute purulent pericarditis from hematogenous seeding of bacteria to the pericardial space

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dressler syndrome, a late post-myocardial infarction pericarditis with autoimmune features

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute hemorrhagic pericarditis from metastatic disease spreading to the pericardial space

choices.label: E
choices.text: Constrictive pericarditis with chronic fibrosis and calcification restricting ventricular filling

explanation.summary: Dressler syndrome is a late post-myocardial infarction pericarditis that occurs weeks after an MI. It presents with pleuritic chest pain, diffuse ST elevation, pericardial effusion, and elevated inflammatory markers, consistent with an autoimmune-mediated fibrinous pericarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dressler syndrome occurs weeks after an MI (as opposed to early infarct-associated pericarditis which occurs within days). It is a form of fibrinous pericarditis with autoimmune features, presenting with pleuritic chest pain that improves with leaning forward, diffuse ST elevation, pericardial effusion, and systemic inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Reinfarction would show ST elevation localized to a specific coronary territory, not diffuse ST elevation. The pleuritic nature of the pain and improvement with leaning forward are characteristic of pericarditis, not ischemic chest pain.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Purulent pericarditis is caused by bacterial infection (commonly S. aureus) and would present with high fevers, systemic toxicity, and purulent pericardial fluid, not the delayed post-MI presentation described here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemorrhagic pericarditis from malignancy would require a history of cancer. This patient has no malignancy history, and the timing after MI points to Dressler syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Constrictive pericarditis is a chronic condition with pericardial fibrosis that impairs ventricular filling. It would not present acutely 3 weeks after MI with diffuse ST elevation.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with a history of systemic lupus erythematosus presents with sudden-onset sharp chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration. Her temperature is 99.5°F. An ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression. Echocardiogram reveals a moderate pericardial effusion with thin, watery, straw-colored fluid. Which of the following pathologic types of acute pericarditis is most consistent with these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fibrinous pericarditis with dense fibrin deposits and prominent exudate on pericardial surfaces

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemorrhagic pericarditis with blood-tinged fluid and malignant cells within the pericardial space

choices.label: C
choices.text: Purulent pericarditis with neutrophil-rich inflammatory infiltrate and bacterial organisms present

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serous pericarditis with minimal pericardial inflammation and a thin watery pericardial effusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Constrictive pericarditis with dense fibrotic thickening and calcification of the pericardial layers

explanation.summary: Serous pericarditis is characterized by thin, watery, straw-colored pericardial effusion with minimal inflammation. It is typically caused by viral infections or autoimmune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thin, watery, straw-colored effusion with minimal inflammation in the setting of SLE is characteristic of serous pericarditis. Autoimmune diseases (including SLE and rheumatoid arthritis) and viral infections are the most common causes of this pathologic subtype.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fibrinous pericarditis features fibrin deposition on pericardial surfaces with significant exudate. It is caused by MI, uremia, trauma, or radiation, not typically by autoimmune diseases.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemorrhagic pericarditis produces bloody pericardial fluid and is associated with malignancies, invasive cardiac procedures, or tuberculosis, not with the thin straw-colored fluid described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Purulent pericarditis produces grossly purulent fluid with neutrophil-rich inflammation and is caused by bacterial infections, most commonly Staphylococcus aureus, not autoimmune diseases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Constrictive pericarditis is a chronic condition with fibrosis and calcification that impairs ventricular filling. It is not an acute pathologic type and would not present with effusion.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of lung cancer presents with progressive dyspnea and chest pain over the past week. On examination, his blood pressure is 88/60 mm Hg, heart rate is 120 beats/min, and jugular venous distension is noted. Heart sounds are distant. Echocardiogram shows a large pericardial effusion with right atrial and ventricular diastolic collapse. Pericardiocentesis is performed, and the aspirated fluid is grossly bloody. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient's pericardial effusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune inflammation from systemic lupus erythematosus causing serous pericardial fluid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Viral infection with coxsackievirus causing serous effusion with minimal pericardial inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uremic toxin accumulation from undiagnosed renal failure causing fibrinous pericardial changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bacterial infection with Staphylococcus aureus producing purulent neutrophil-rich pericardial fluid

choices.label: E
choices.text: Metastatic malignancy spreading to the pericardium causing hemorrhagic pericardial effusion

explanation.summary: Hemorrhagic pericarditis is characterized by bloody pericardial effusion. The most common causes are malignancies that metastasize to the pericardium (such as lung cancer) and invasive cardiac procedures. This patient's lung cancer history and grossly bloody aspirate point to metastatic pericardial involvement.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of a history of lung cancer, cardiac tamponade, and grossly hemorrhagic pericardial fluid is classic for hemorrhagic pericarditis caused by metastatic malignancy. Lung and breast cancers are the most common malignancies to metastasize to the pericardium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SLE causes serous pericarditis with thin, watery, straw-colored fluid, not grossly bloody fluid. There is no history suggesting autoimmune disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Viral pericarditis (e.g., coxsackievirus) causes serous pericarditis with minimal inflammation and watery effusion, not hemorrhagic fluid.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Uremia causes fibrinous pericarditis, which is characterized by fibrin deposition rather than a hemorrhagic effusion. There is no indication of renal failure in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Staphylococcus aureus causes purulent pericarditis with grossly purulent (pus-filled) fluid, not hemorrhagic fluid. The patient's lung cancer history makes malignancy a far more likely cause.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute chest pain and fever. She recently completed a course of antibiotics for pneumonia. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression. Echocardiogram shows a small pericardial effusion. She is diagnosed with acute idiopathic pericarditis and started on appropriate first-line therapy. Two weeks later, she returns with recurrence of symptoms. Which of the following medications should be added to reduce the risk of further recurrences?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prednisone, a corticosteroid that should be used as first-line therapy for all recurrent cases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methotrexate, an immunosuppressant that inhibits folic acid metabolism in inflammatory cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Azathioprine, an immunosuppressant that inhibits purine synthesis to reduce inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Colchicine, an anti-inflammatory agent that has been shown to reduce pericarditis recurrence

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hydroxychloroquine, an antimalarial drug that modulates the immune response in autoimmune disease

explanation.summary: Colchicine is used as an adjunct to NSAID therapy in acute pericarditis and has been shown to reduce the recurrence of pericarditis. It can be continued for months after the pericarditis resolves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Colchicine has been demonstrated to reduce the recurrence of pericarditis and can be used as an adjunct to NSAID therapy for both acute viral and idiopathic pericarditis. It can be used for months after the pericarditis resolves to prevent future episodes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Corticosteroids should not be used as initial treatment unless NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated or the patient has not responded to them. They are not recommended as first-line for recurrence prevention.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate is not part of the standard management algorithm for acute pericarditis or its recurrence as described in current guidelines for this condition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Azathioprine is not included in the standard treatment approach for acute pericarditis recurrence. The established agents are NSAIDs, colchicine, and corticosteroids as a last resort.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hydroxychloroquine, while used in autoimmune conditions, is not part of the standard management for idiopathic acute pericarditis recurrence.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with no significant medical history presents to the emergency department with fever and severe chest pain for 2 days. He reports the pain is sharp and radiates to his left shoulder. On examination, his temperature is 102.2°F, blood pressure is 128/78 mm Hg, and heart rate is 96 beats/min. Cardiac auscultation reveals a high-pitched scratchy sound heard best at the left sternal border. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression. Echocardiogram is normal without effusion. Which of the following diagnostic findings confirmed the diagnosis even before the ECG was obtained?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The elevated temperature indicating a systemic infectious or inflammatory process present

choices.label: B
choices.text: The pericardial friction rub heard on cardiac auscultation during the physical examination

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sharp chest pain radiating to the left shoulder suggesting pericardial nerve irritation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The normal echocardiogram without effusion, which rules out other cardiac pathology

choices.label: E
choices.text: The absence of prior cardiac history making ischemic heart disease an unlikely explanation

explanation.summary: The four principal diagnostic features of acute pericarditis are chest pain, pericardial friction rub, typical ECG findings, and pericardial effusion on echocardiogram. A pericardial friction rub is a classic physical examination finding that can confirm the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A pericardial friction rub is one of the four principal diagnostic features of acute pericarditis. It is a superficial scratchy sound produced when inflamed pericardial layers slide past each other and can be detected during the physical examination before any imaging or ECG is obtained.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While fever can be associated with acute pericarditis (especially infectious causes), fever alone is nonspecific and is not one of the four principal diagnostic criteria for acute pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While sharp pleuritic chest pain is a diagnostic feature, chest pain alone could represent many conditions. The question asks which finding confirmed the diagnosis, and the friction rub is more specific.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A normal echocardiogram does not confirm pericarditis. While effusion on echocardiogram is one diagnostic feature, its absence does not rule in or out the diagnosis, as the echocardiogram is typically normal when no effusion is present.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The absence of prior cardiac history does not confirm any diagnosis. It merely makes certain conditions less likely but does not positively establish pericarditis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with chest pain, fever, and rigors for 4 days. She recently underwent cardiac surgery for valve replacement. On examination, her temperature is 103.5°F, blood pressure is 100/64 mm Hg, and heart rate is 115 beats/min. Echocardiogram shows a moderate pericardial effusion. Blood cultures are drawn and grow Staphylococcus aureus. The team is concerned about purulent pericarditis. Which of the following is the most appropriate procedural intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiac MRI to assess for pericardial inflammation and guide medical management of the infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: CT scan of the chest to evaluate for pericardial thickening and assess the size of effusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pericardiocentesis to aspirate fluid for culture and cytology and relieve pericardial pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serial echocardiography to monitor the effusion size and wait for antibiotics to take effect

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coronary angiography to evaluate for coronary artery disease causing ischemic chest pain signs

explanation.summary: In suspected purulent (bacterial) pericarditis, pericardiocentesis is indicated for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. The aspirated fluid can be sent for cultures and cytology, and the procedure relieves pressure from the effusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pericardiocentesis is specifically indicated when purulent pericarditis is suspected or when there is cardiac tamponade or impending tamponade. It serves both diagnostic (culture and cytology of aspirated fluid) and therapeutic (fluid removal and pressure relief) purposes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While cardiac MRI can detect pericardial inflammation, it is an imaging study, not a procedural intervention. In suspected purulent pericarditis, pericardiocentesis provides both diagnosis and treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CT scan is a diagnostic imaging modality that can show pericardial thickening and effusion but does not provide the therapeutic benefit of fluid drainage or the diagnostic benefit of fluid analysis needed in suspected purulent pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serial monitoring without intervention is inappropriate when purulent pericarditis is suspected due to the risk of rapid clinical deterioration, tamponade, and sepsis. Pericardiocentesis is needed for source control.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coronary angiography evaluates for coronary artery disease and is not relevant to the management of purulent pericarditis following cardiac surgery with positive blood cultures.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman who completed radiation therapy for breast cancer 6 months ago presents with chest pain that worsens when she lies down and improves when she leans forward. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation. An echocardiogram reveals a small pericardial effusion. She is started on ibuprofen, but after 1 week, her symptoms persist with no improvement. Colchicine is added, but after another 2 weeks, she continues to have significant chest pain and inflammatory markers remain elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue the same NSAID and colchicine regimen for an additional 4 weeks before reassessment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate corticosteroid therapy for refractory pericarditis unresponsive to standard treatment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform urgent pericardiocentesis to remove the small effusion causing persistent symptoms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy for possible undiagnosed bacterial pericarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Refer for immediate pericardiectomy as definitive surgical treatment for the chronic condition

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids can be used in acute pericarditis when the patient has not responded to NSAIDs or colchicine, or when these medications are contraindicated. This patient has failed both first-line (NSAIDs) and adjunctive (colchicine) therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Corticosteroids should not be used for initial treatment of pericarditis, but they can be used if the patient has not responded to NSAIDs or colchicine, or if these agents are contraindicated. After failing both NSAID and colchicine therapy over several weeks, corticosteroids are the appropriate next step.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Continuing the same failing regimen without escalation is inappropriate when the patient has persistent symptoms and elevated inflammatory markers after adequate trial of both NSAIDs and colchicine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pericardiocentesis is reserved for cases of cardiac tamponade, impending tamponade, or suspected purulent pericarditis. A small effusion without hemodynamic compromise does not warrant this invasive procedure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no clinical evidence of bacterial (purulent) pericarditis in this patient. Her presentation is consistent with radiation-induced pericarditis, and empiric antibiotics are not indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pericardiectomy is a treatment for constrictive pericarditis, not acute pericarditis that has been refractory to first-line medications. Medical escalation to corticosteroids should be attempted before considering surgery.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents to the emergency department with chest pain that started yesterday. The pain is worse with inspiration and when lying flat. His ECG shows changes consistent with acute pericarditis. The emergency physician explains to the patient that the ECG changes in pericarditis differ from those seen in acute myocardial infarction. Which of the following ECG characteristics is most specific for distinguishing acute pericarditis from acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ST-segment elevation that is diffuse across multiple leads rather than localized to a vascular territory

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of pathological Q waves indicating transmural myocardial necrosis in contiguous leads

choices.label: C
choices.text: T-wave inversions that develop in leads corresponding to the affected myocardial wall segment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with heart rate above 100 beats per minute reflecting sympathetic activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prolongation of the QT interval indicating delayed ventricular repolarization from electrolyte shift

explanation.summary: In acute pericarditis, ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation (and PR-segment depression) across multiple leads, reflecting generalized pericardial inflammation. In contrast, STEMI shows ST elevation localized to leads corresponding to a specific coronary artery territory.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diffuse ST-segment elevation across multiple leads is the hallmark ECG finding that distinguishes acute pericarditis from STEMI. Pericardial inflammation is generalized, affecting the entire pericardium, so ECG changes appear in multiple leads rather than being confined to a single vascular territory.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pathological Q waves are a feature of myocardial infarction (indicating transmural necrosis), not pericarditis. Their presence would suggest MI rather than help diagnose pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: T-wave inversions in leads corresponding to an affected wall segment are characteristic of myocardial ischemia or infarction, not pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus tachycardia is a nonspecific finding that can be present in many conditions including both pericarditis and MI, and does not distinguish between them.
whyWrongByChoice.E: QT prolongation is associated with electrolyte abnormalities or certain medications and is not a distinguishing feature of either pericarditis or myocardial infarction.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with acute pericarditis develops progressive dyspnea and hypotension over 48 hours. Echocardiogram shows a rapidly accumulating pericardial effusion with diastolic collapse of the right atrium and right ventricle. His blood pressure is 82/58 mm Hg with pulsus paradoxus of 18 mm Hg. Neck veins are distended and heart sounds are muffled. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of his hemodynamic deterioration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pericardial fluid is compressing the coronary arteries, leading to diffuse myocardial ischemia and failure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pericardial inflammation has extended into the myocardium, causing acute viral myocarditis and failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased pericardial pressure is compressing the cardiac chambers, compromising ventricular filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pericardial fibrosis has developed acutely, constricting the heart and preventing adequate diastolic filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inflammatory mediators have caused systemic vasodilation, leading to distributive shock physiology

explanation.summary: Cardiac tamponade is a complication of acute pericarditis in which sufficient pericardial fluid accumulation increases pressure in the pericardial space, compressing the cardiac chambers and compromising cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cardiac tamponade, the accumulating pericardial effusion raises intrapericardial pressure, which compresses the cardiac chambers (particularly the thin-walled right atrium and ventricle first). This prevents adequate ventricular filling during diastole, reducing stroke volume and cardiac output.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pericardial fluid does not compress the coronary arteries. The hemodynamic compromise results from compression of the cardiac chambers preventing filling, not from coronary ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While myopericarditis can occur, cardiac tamponade results from mechanical compression by pericardial fluid, not from extension of inflammation into the myocardium causing myocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericardial fibrosis causing constriction (constrictive pericarditis) is a chronic complication that develops over weeks to months, not acutely over 48 hours. The acute hemodynamic deterioration here is from fluid accumulation, not fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The hemodynamic deterioration in tamponade is from obstructive physiology (mechanical compression), not distributive physiology (vasodilation). The elevated neck veins and muffled heart sounds are characteristic of obstructive rather than distributive shock.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with acute chest pain that is pleuritic in nature and worsens when she lies supine. She had a viral upper respiratory infection 10 days ago. Her temperature is 100.4°F. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression. Echocardiogram shows a trivial pericardial effusion without hemodynamic compromise. Troponin is mildly elevated. She is hemodynamically stable. The physician plans to start treatment. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids to rapidly suppress the pericardial inflammatory response

choices.label: B
choices.text: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics to cover potential bacterial pathogens causing the pericarditis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticoagulation with heparin to prevent thrombus formation within the pericardial effusion present

choices.label: D
choices.text: Urgent pericardiocentesis to remove the trivial effusion and prevent progression to tamponade

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs as primary treatment to relieve pain and resolve inflammation

explanation.summary: NSAIDs are the primary first-line treatment for acute pericarditis. They address the therapeutic goals of relieving chest pain, resolving inflammation, and preventing recurrence. Corticosteroids should not be used initially unless NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated.
explanation.whyCorrect: NSAIDs are the primary treatment for acute pericarditis. In this patient with presumed viral or idiopathic pericarditis (preceded by a viral URI), NSAIDs are appropriate first-line therapy to relieve chest pain and resolve inflammation. Colchicine may be added as an adjunct.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Corticosteroids should not be used for initial treatment of acute pericarditis unless they are indicated for the underlying disease or unless NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated or have failed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This presentation is most consistent with viral or idiopathic pericarditis following a recent URI. There is no clinical evidence of bacterial (purulent) pericarditis, so empiric antibiotics are not indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticoagulation is not part of the management of acute pericarditis. In fact, it could potentially worsen a pericardial effusion by promoting hemorrhagic transformation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericardiocentesis is only indicated for cardiac tamponade, impending tamponade, or suspected purulent pericarditis. A trivial effusion without hemodynamic compromise does not warrant this invasive procedure.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the cardiology clinic with a 6-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea, lower-extremity swelling, and abdominal distention. He underwent mediastinal radiation therapy 8 years ago for Hodgkin lymphoma and has been in remission since. On physical examination, his jugular venous pressure is markedly elevated and does not decrease with inspiration. Auscultation reveals a high-pitched early diastolic sound best heard at the left sternal border. Echocardiography shows a thickened pericardium with abnormal interventricular septal motion during respiration. Which of the following hemodynamic findings is most likely to be observed on cardiac catheterization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Equalization of end-diastolic pressures across all four cardiac chambers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Markedly elevated left ventricular systolic pressure with normal diastolic pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Significant pressure gradient between the right atrium and right ventricle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isolated elevation of left atrial pressure with normal right-sided pressures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased right atrial pressure with prominent a wave on venous tracing

explanation.summary: Constrictive pericarditis causes a fibrotic, inelastic pericardium that restricts ventricular filling. Cardiac catheterization, the gold standard for diagnosis, characteristically demonstrates elevation and equalization of end-diastolic pressures in all four cardiac chambers.
explanation.whyCorrect: In constrictive pericarditis, the fibrosed pericardium exerts equal pressure on all chambers, causing end-diastolic pressures to become elevated and equalized. This equalization is a hallmark catheterization finding and occurs because the stiff pericardium prevents normal pressure differences between chambers during diastole.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The primary hemodynamic derangement in constrictive pericarditis is impaired diastolic filling, not systolic dysfunction. Diastolic pressures are characteristically elevated, not normal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: In constrictive pericarditis, pressures equalize across chambers rather than showing significant gradients. A significant gradient between RA and RV would suggest tricuspid stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Constrictive pericarditis affects all chambers equally due to the circumferential nature of pericardial constriction, so isolated left-sided pressure elevation with normal right-sided pressures would not be expected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Right atrial pressure is elevated, not decreased, in constrictive pericarditis. The characteristic venous tracing finding is a prominent y descent, reflecting rapid early diastolic emptying of the right atrium.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man from sub-Saharan Africa presents with progressive fatigue, bilateral leg swelling, and increasing abdominal girth over the past 4 months. He has a history of a pulmonary infection treated with a prolonged antibiotic regimen 3 years ago. Physical examination shows jugular venous distention, hepatomegaly, ascites, and bilateral pitting edema. On cardiac auscultation, a distinct early diastolic sound is heard that is high-pitched and best described as a tapping noise. Echocardiography demonstrates pericardial thickening with restricted ventricular filling. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune connective tissue disorder causing pericardial inflammation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tuberculosis leading to chronic pericardial fibrosis and constriction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prior viral myocarditis resulting in dilated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Idiopathic pericardial fibrosis with no identifiable underlying cause

choices.label: E
choices.text: Post-surgical adhesion formation following prior cardiac surgery

explanation.summary: In developing countries, tuberculosis is the leading cause of constrictive pericarditis. This patient from sub-Saharan Africa with a history of prior pulmonary infection and classic findings of constrictive pericarditis most likely has tuberculous constrictive pericarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tuberculosis is the leading cause of constrictive pericarditis in developing countries and should always be considered in the differential diagnosis. The patient's geographic origin, prior pulmonary infection treated with prolonged antibiotics (consistent with TB treatment), and classic constrictive findings strongly point to tuberculosis as the etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While connective tissue disorders can cause constrictive pericarditis, they are less common than tuberculosis in developing countries. No systemic features of autoimmune disease are described in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Viral myocarditis causes dilated cardiomyopathy with systolic dysfunction, not the diastolic filling impairment and pericardial thickening seen in constrictive pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Idiopathic constrictive pericarditis is more commonly diagnosed in developed countries. Given this patient's geographic origin and history of prior pulmonary infection, a specific etiology (TB) is far more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Post-surgical constrictive pericarditis occurs after cardiac surgery. There is no history of cardiac surgery in this patient.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer treated with thoracic radiotherapy 10 years ago presents with worsening dyspnea, fatigue, and peripheral edema. On examination, her systolic blood pressure drops from 132 mm Hg to 118 mm Hg during deep inspiration. Jugular venous distention is present, and the JVP rises paradoxically during inspiration. A high-pitched early diastolic sound is heard on auscultation. Which of the following best explains why this patient's jugular venous pressure increases rather than decreases during inspiration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe tricuspid regurgitation causing retrograde blood flow during systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance impeding right ventricular outflow

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fibrosed pericardium prevents transmission of decreased intrathoracic pressure to the heart

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left ventricular systolic failure causing elevated pulmonary capillary wedge pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Compression of the superior vena cava by mediastinal lymphadenopathy

explanation.summary: Kussmaul sign is the paradoxical increase (or lack of normal decrease) of JVP during inspiration. In constrictive pericarditis, the fibrosed pericardium prevents the normal transmission of negative intrathoracic pressure to the heart, so venous return does not increase appropriately and JVP rises.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally, inspiration generates negative intrathoracic pressure that is transmitted to the heart and great veins, lowering right atrial pressure and increasing the pressure gradient for venous return. In constrictive pericarditis, the rigid, fibrotic pericardium insulates the heart from these intrathoracic pressure changes, preventing venous return from increasing during inspiration and causing JVP to rise (Kussmaul sign).

whyWrongByChoice.A: While tricuspid regurgitation can cause Kussmaul sign, this patient's presentation with post-radiation pericardial thickening, pericardial knock, and pulsus paradoxus is most consistent with constrictive pericarditis, not primary valvular disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased pulmonary vascular resistance would impede right ventricular outflow but would not directly explain the Kussmaul sign mechanism. The fundamental issue is the noncompliant pericardium, not pulmonary hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left ventricular systolic failure is not the mechanism in constrictive pericarditis, which is primarily a diastolic filling disorder caused by an inelastic pericardium rather than myocardial dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SVC compression by lymphadenopathy could cause elevated JVP but would not produce the constellation of pericardial knock, pulsus paradoxus, and pericardial thickening seen in this patient.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of recurrent pericarditis is being evaluated for progressively worsening exertional dyspnea, bilateral pleural effusions, and ascites. Echocardiography reveals increased pericardial thickness and an abnormal interventricular septal bounce during respiration. During inspiration, echocardiographic assessment shows that the right ventricular volume increases while the left ventricular volume markedly decreases. Which of the following best describes the mechanism underlying the inspiratory decrease in left ventricular volume in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced pulmonary venous return due to increased pulmonary vascular resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mitral valve stenosis preventing adequate left ventricular filling during diastole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve causing left ventricular outflow obstruction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhanced ventricular interdependence causing the interventricular septum to shift leftward

choices.label: E
choices.text: Paradoxical relaxation of the left ventricular myocardium during the inspiratory phase

explanation.summary: Enhanced ventricular interdependence is a hallmark of constrictive pericarditis. The inelastic pericardium cannot accommodate the increased right ventricular volume during inspiration, so the interventricular septum shifts leftward, markedly reducing left ventricular filling and volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: In constrictive pericarditis, the rigid pericardium cannot expand. During inspiration, increased venous return fills the right ventricle, but the inelastic pericardium forces the interventricular septum to bulge leftward to accommodate this volume, markedly decreasing LV filling. This enhanced ventricular interdependence reduces stroke volume and cardiac output during inspiration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Reduced pulmonary venous return from increased pulmonary vascular resistance is not the primary mechanism. The key pathology is the rigid pericardium causing enhanced interventricular dependence, not changes in pulmonary vasculature.
whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no mitral valve stenosis in this case. The impaired LV filling is due to mechanical compression from septal shift caused by the noncompliant pericardium, not a valvular lesion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve is a feature of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, not constrictive pericarditis. The pathology here involves diastolic filling impairment, not outflow obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paradoxical relaxation of the LV myocardium is not a recognized mechanism in constrictive pericarditis. The LV myocardium itself functions normally; the problem is external constraint from the fibrotic pericardium.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a history of idiopathic constrictive pericarditis confirmed by echocardiography presents with worsening symptoms of dyspnea at rest, severe peripheral edema, and refractory ascites despite maximal medical therapy with diuretics. His cardiac output remains significantly reduced. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiation of high-dose corticosteroid therapy for immunosuppression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Addition of an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor to the regimen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implantation of an automated implantable cardioverter-defibrillator

choices.label: D
choices.text: Repeat echocardiography with Doppler assessment in 3 months

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pericardiectomy with careful preoperative risk assessment

explanation.summary: Pericardiectomy is the only definitive treatment for constrictive pericarditis in patients who are markedly symptomatic and refractory to medical therapy. Although operative mortality is significant (4-12%), it is indicated for patients with severe, progressive symptoms.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has severe, symptomatic constrictive pericarditis refractory to medical management. Pericardiectomy, the surgical removal of part or all of the pericardium, is the definitive treatment for such patients. While operative risk is high (4-12% operative mortality), it often improves symptoms in carefully selected patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fibrotic tissue, which is the hallmark of constrictive pericarditis, does not respond well to anti-inflammatory drugs or corticosteroids. These may only be useful if there is concurrent active acute pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ACE inhibitors address afterload reduction but do not correct the fundamental problem of external cardiac constriction by fibrotic pericardium. They cannot improve the impaired diastolic filling caused by the rigid pericardium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An ICD is indicated for patients at risk of ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death, not for constrictive pericarditis. The patient's symptoms are due to impaired diastolic filling, not arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Watchful waiting with repeat imaging is inappropriate in a patient with severe, refractory symptoms and significantly reduced cardiac output. Definitive surgical intervention is needed.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with constrictive pericarditis is started on diuretic therapy for management of his peripheral edema and ascites. Two days later, he develops lightheadedness and near-syncope. His blood pressure is 82/54 mm Hg, and his heart rate is 104 beats/min. Which of the following best explains why this patient's hemodynamic status has worsened after initiation of diuretic therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The reduction in intravascular volume further decreased cardiac output in an already constricted heart

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diuretic-induced hypokalemia has caused a significant cardiac arrhythmia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diuretics have increased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure leading to pulmonary edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug-induced pericardial inflammation has acutely worsened the degree of constriction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diuretic therapy has paradoxically increased venous return by decreasing systemic vascular resistance

explanation.summary: In constrictive pericarditis, the heart already has a low cardiac output due to compression by fibrotic tissue. Diuretics reduce intravascular volume, which further lowers preload and cardiac output, potentially causing hypotension. Diuretics must be used with caution in these patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: The fibrotic pericardium compresses the heart, already limiting ventricular filling and reducing cardiac output. When diuretics lower intravascular volume (preload), the already compromised heart cannot compensate, leading to further decreases in cardiac output and hypotension. This is why diuretics must be used with caution in constrictive pericarditis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While diuretic-induced hypokalemia can cause arrhythmias, this scenario specifically illustrates the well-known risk of volume depletion in constrictive pericarditis. The hemodynamic deterioration is best explained by reduced preload in a constricted heart.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diuretics decrease intravascular volume and therefore decrease pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, not increase it. They are used specifically to reduce congestion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diuretics do not cause pericardial inflammation. Drug-induced pericarditis is not a recognized side effect of standard diuretics like furosemide.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diuretics reduce intravascular volume; they do not decrease systemic vascular resistance or paradoxically increase venous return. The hemodynamic compromise is due to decreased preload.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with progressively worsening exercise intolerance and bilateral lower-extremity edema undergoes evaluation for suspected constrictive pericarditis. An ECG is obtained as part of the workup. Which of the following ECG findings would be most consistent with constrictive pericarditis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ST-segment elevation in leads I, II, III, aVF, and V3-V6 with PR depression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tall peaked T waves with widened QRS complexes in all leads

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low QRS voltages with generalized T-wave flattening or inversion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left bundle branch block pattern with left axis deviation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pathologic Q waves in leads V1-V4 with persistent ST elevation

explanation.summary: The ECG in constrictive pericarditis is often nonspecific but may show low QRS voltages and generalized T-wave flattening or inversion. Some patients may also have atrial fibrillation. No uniquely identifying ECG features exist for this condition.
explanation.whyCorrect: ECG in constrictive pericarditis generally does not reveal uniquely identifying features and may even be normal. Nonspecific changes that may be present include low QRS voltages and generalized T-wave flattening or inversion. Some patients may also exhibit atrial fibrillation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression is the classic ECG pattern of acute pericarditis, not constrictive pericarditis, which is a chronic fibrotic process.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tall peaked T waves with widened QRS complexes suggest hyperkalemia, not constrictive pericarditis. Constrictive pericarditis typically shows low (not high) QRS voltages.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left bundle branch block with left axis deviation suggests conduction system disease or structural heart disease such as cardiomyopathy, not constrictive pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pathologic Q waves with persistent ST elevation in anterior leads suggest a prior anterior myocardial infarction with possible ventricular aneurysm, not constrictive pericarditis.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man who recently immigrated from Southeast Asia presents with a 3-month history of increasing abdominal girth, bilateral lower-extremity swelling, and dyspnea on exertion. Physical examination reveals elevated JVP with a prominent y descent, hepatomegaly, and ascites. A chest x-ray is obtained. Which of the following chest x-ray findings, if present, would most strongly suggest constrictive pericarditis as the underlying etiology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with diffuse interstitial infiltrates

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enlarged cardiac silhouette with a globular water-bottle configuration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Widened mediastinum with leftward tracheal deviation and pleural capping

choices.label: D
choices.text: Curvilinear calcification surrounding the cardiac silhouette

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bilateral apical cavitary lesions with surrounding fibrotic changes

explanation.summary: Chest x-ray in constrictive pericarditis is usually normal but may demonstrate pericardial calcium deposits in a minority of patients. Curvilinear calcifications surrounding the heart are characteristic when present.
explanation.whyCorrect: While chest x-ray is usually normal in constrictive pericarditis, the most suggestive finding is pericardial calcium deposits, appearing as curvilinear calcifications surrounding the cardiac silhouette. This represents calcification of the chronically inflamed and fibrosed pericardium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with interstitial infiltrates suggests sarcoidosis or lymphoma, not constrictive pericarditis. While these conditions can rarely involve the pericardium, this radiographic pattern is not characteristic of constriction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A globular water-bottle cardiac silhouette is the classic chest x-ray finding of a large pericardial effusion, not constrictive pericarditis. In constriction, the heart size is typically normal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A widened mediastinum with tracheal deviation suggests aortic pathology (e.g., aortic aneurysm or dissection) or mediastinal mass, not constrictive pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bilateral apical cavitary lesions suggest active pulmonary tuberculosis. While TB is a leading cause of constrictive pericarditis in developing countries, cavitary lesions represent pulmonary disease, not the pericardial calcification characteristic of constrictive pericarditis.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman with a history of systemic lupus erythematosus presents with worsening dyspnea, ascites, and lower-extremity edema. On examination, her JVP is elevated and a high-pitched early diastolic sound is heard at the left sternal border. She is diagnosed with constrictive pericarditis. Her cardiologist explains that her cardiac output is reduced due to impaired ventricular filling. Which of the following phases of the cardiac cycle is most directly impaired by the fibrosed pericardium in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Passive ventricular filling and atrial contraction during diastole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid ejection phase of the ventricles during mid-systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isovolumetric relaxation of the ventricles during early diastole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Isovolumetric contraction of the ventricles during early systole

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial depolarization and initiation of the atrial contraction phase

explanation.summary: Constrictive pericarditis primarily impairs diastolic filling of the ventricles. The fibrosed, inelastic pericardium restricts ventricular expansion during diastole, limiting the volume of blood that can enter the chambers during both passive filling and atrial contraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hallmark of constrictive pericarditis is impaired diastolic filling. The fibrosed pericardium reaches its elastic limit early in diastole, causing abrupt cessation of ventricular filling (producing the pericardial knock). Both passive filling and the contribution of atrial contraction are impaired because the ventricles cannot expand beyond the constraints of the rigid pericardium.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The rapid ejection phase involves ventricular emptying, not filling. The pericardium primarily restricts ventricular expansion (filling), not contraction or ejection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While isovolumetric relaxation precedes filling, it is the filling phase itself that is directly impaired. During isovolumetric relaxation, ventricular volume does not change, so pericardial restriction has minimal direct effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Isovolumetric contraction occurs with all valves closed and involves no volume change. The fibrosed pericardium does not primarily impair this phase since no ventricular expansion occurs during systole.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial depolarization is an electrical event that initiates atrial contraction. The fibrosed pericardium does not impair the electrical conduction system; it mechanically restricts ventricular expansion during filling.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with chronic constrictive pericarditis is being evaluated for worsening symptoms. On physical examination, a high-pitched early diastolic sound is auscultated. The sound occurs earlier in diastole than the third heart sound (S3) heard in heart failure. Which of the following best describes the mechanism producing this auscultatory finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Turbulent blood flow across a stenotic mitral valve during ventricular filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vibration of a calcified aortic valve during ventricular ejection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Retrograde blood flow through an incompetent aortic valve in early diastole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abrupt cessation of ventricular filling when the pericardial elastic limit is reached

choices.label: E
choices.text: Snapping of a prolapsing mitral valve leaflet during ventricular systole

explanation.summary: A pericardial knock is a high-pitched early diastolic sound caused by the abrupt cessation of ventricular filling as the ventricles reach the elastic limit of the constrictive pericardium. It occurs earlier in diastole than a typical S3.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pericardial knock is produced when the rapidly filling ventricles abruptly hit the noncompliant, fibrosed pericardium and suddenly stop expanding. This abrupt halt in ventricular filling generates the high-pitched early diastolic tapping sound characteristic of constrictive pericarditis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Turbulent flow across a stenotic mitral valve produces a low-pitched diastolic rumble (mitral stenosis murmur), not the high-pitched knock heard in constrictive pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vibration of a calcified aortic valve during ejection produces a systolic murmur (aortic stenosis), not an early diastolic sound.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Retrograde flow through an incompetent aortic valve produces an early diastolic decrescendo blowing murmur (aortic regurgitation), which has a different character than the brief, discrete pericardial knock.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A prolapsing mitral valve produces a mid-systolic click, not an early diastolic sound. This finding is associated with mitral valve prolapse, not pericardial disease.

questions.stem: A 49-year-old man presents with dyspnea on exertion, peripheral edema, and ascites. Physical examination reveals elevated JVP with a prominent y descent, Kussmaul sign, and a pericardial knock. Echocardiography shows pericardial thickening but the results are equivocal for constrictive physiology. Which of the following is the gold standard for confirming the diagnosis of constrictive pericarditis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging with late gadolinium enhancement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endomyocardial biopsy with histopathological analysis of the myocardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serum brain natriuretic peptide level with serial troponin measurements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Invasive hemodynamic evaluation through cardiac catheterization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transesophageal echocardiography with intravenous contrast administration

explanation.summary: When constrictive pericarditis is highly suspected and echocardiography is equivocal, invasive hemodynamic evaluation through cardiac catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosis. It can directly demonstrate equalization of diastolic pressures and exaggerated ventricular interdependence.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing constrictive pericarditis. Using pressure catheters inside the right and left ventricles, characteristic hemodynamic changes can be directly observed, including elevation and equalization of diastolic blood pressures, exaggerated ventricular interdependence, elevated right atrial pressure, and a prominent y descent.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While cardiac MRI can be used as an alternative to echocardiography for anatomical visualization and surgical planning, it is not the gold standard for confirming constrictive physiology. Hemodynamic evaluation provides definitive functional assessment.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Endomyocardial biopsy evaluates myocardial tissue and is used for diagnosing conditions like myocarditis or infiltrative cardiomyopathies. It does not assess the pericardial constriction or hemodynamic changes of constrictive pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BNP and troponin are serum biomarkers that can be elevated in various cardiac conditions but are not diagnostic for constrictive pericarditis and cannot demonstrate the characteristic hemodynamic findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While transesophageal echocardiography provides better imaging than transthoracic echocardiography, it is not the gold standard. Cardiac catheterization provides direct hemodynamic measurements that definitively confirm constrictive physiology.

questions.stem: A 51-year-old man with known constrictive pericarditis secondary to prior viral pericarditis is being managed medically. His physician explains that the decreased venous return in constrictive pericarditis further contributes to his reduced cardiac output. During inspiration, the patient's JVP is noted to increase. Which of the following best describes how decreased venous return occurs in constrictive pericarditis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe mitral regurgitation reduces effective forward flow, causing pulmonary venous congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased pericardial compliance allows excessive right atrial filling, reducing the venous pressure gradient

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fibrosed pericardium blocks transmission of negative intrathoracic pressure to the right atrium, abolishing the thoracic pump effect

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic elevation of aldosterone levels leads to profound sodium and water retention with volume overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Calcification of the coronary arteries reduces myocardial blood supply, impairing atrial contractile function

explanation.summary: In constrictive pericarditis, the fibrosed pericardium prevents the normal transmission of negative intrathoracic pressure to the heart during inspiration. This abolishes the normal thoracic pump mechanism, reducing the pressure gradient between extrathoracic veins and the right atrium, and decreasing venous return.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally, inspiration creates negative intrathoracic pressure that is transmitted to the right atrium, increasing the pressure gradient between extrathoracic veins and the right atrium (the thoracic pump). In constrictive pericarditis, the rigid pericardium prevents this pressure transmission, so the gradient does not increase and venous return drops, leading to venous congestion and reduced preload.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mitral regurgitation is not a feature of constrictive pericarditis. The reduced cardiac output in constrictive pericarditis is due to impaired diastolic filling from external pericardial compression, not valvular insufficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: In constrictive pericarditis, pericardial compliance is decreased (not increased) due to fibrosis. The decreased compliance is the fundamental problem that prevents normal cardiac filling and venous return.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While neurohormonal activation may occur as a compensatory response in constrictive pericarditis, it is not the primary mechanism of decreased venous return. The fundamental issue is the mechanical effect of the rigid pericardium on intracardiac pressure transmission.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pericardial calcification in constrictive pericarditis does not affect coronary artery blood flow. The calcification is in the pericardium, not the coronary arteries, and atrial contractile dysfunction is not the mechanism of reduced venous return.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after sustaining a stab wound to the left anterior chest. She is diaphoretic and appears pale. Her blood pressure is 76/52 mm Hg, heart rate is 118 beats/min, and respiratory rate is 24 breaths/min. Her neck veins are distended, and heart sounds are difficult to hear. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of this patient's hemodynamic compromise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid accumulation of blood in the pericardial space increases pericardial pressure, compressing the right-sided chambers and reducing cardiac output

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct myocardial laceration has caused severe systolic dysfunction of the left ventricle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tension pneumothorax has caused mediastinal shift with compression of the great vessels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hemorrhage into the pleural space has caused hypovolemic shock from blood loss

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coronary artery laceration has resulted in acute transmural myocardial infarction

explanation.summary: Penetrating chest trauma can cause hemopericardium, where blood rapidly accumulates in the pericardial space. As little as 100 mL of acutely accumulated blood can cause tamponade. The elevated pericardial pressure compresses the low-pressure right atrium and right ventricle first, reducing venous return, LV preload, and cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: In acute cardiac tamponade from penetrating trauma, rapid hemorrhage into the pericardial space elevates intrapericardial pressure. This first compresses the low-pressure right atrium and right ventricle, decreasing venous return and LV preload. The result is significantly reduced cardiac output and hypotension, with jugular venous distention and muffled heart sounds (Beck triad).

whyWrongByChoice.B: While direct myocardial injury is possible, the triad of hypotension, distended neck veins, and muffled heart sounds specifically points to cardiac tamponade rather than isolated myocardial laceration, which would more likely present with hemorrhagic shock without JVD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tension pneumothorax can also cause hypotension and JVD, but it typically presents with absent breath sounds on the affected side, tracheal deviation, and hyperresonance to percussion. Muffled heart sounds specifically suggest pericardial fluid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypovolemic shock from hemothorax would present with flat (not distended) neck veins due to decreased intravascular volume. The distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds indicate obstructive shock from tamponade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute MI from coronary artery laceration would not explain the rapid onset of muffled heart sounds and JVD. Tamponade physiology from hemopericardium is the most likely mechanism in penetrating chest trauma with this presentation.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer develops progressive dyspnea and fatigue over 3 weeks. On examination, his blood pressure is 88/60 mm Hg, heart rate is 108 beats/min, and jugular veins are distended. Heart sounds are muffled. An ECG shows beat-to-beat alternation in QRS complex amplitude. Which of the following ECG findings is being described, and what is its mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low-voltage QRS complexes caused by pericardial fluid attenuating electrical conduction to the ECG leads

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrical alternans caused by the swinging motion of the heart within the fluid-filled pericardial sac

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diffuse ST-segment elevation caused by transmural myocardial inflammation from pericarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia caused by sympathetic activation in response to decreased cardiac output

choices.label: E
choices.text: PR-segment depression caused by atrial injury current from pericardial inflammation

explanation.summary: Electrical alternans is the most specific ECG finding for cardiac tamponade. It manifests as beat-to-beat variation in QRS complex amplitude and is caused by the swinging movement of the heart within the fluid-filled pericardial sac, though it is only seen in 20-25% of tamponade cases.
explanation.whyCorrect: The beat-to-beat alternation in QRS complex amplitude is electrical alternans, the most specific ECG finding for cardiac tamponade. It is produced by the swinging movement of the heart within the large pericardial effusion, causing the heart's electrical axis to shift with each beat. However, it is only present in 20-25% of patients with tamponade.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Low-voltage QRS complexes can occur with pericardial effusion as fluid attenuates electrical conduction, but this is a different finding than the beat-to-beat amplitude alternation described. Low voltage is also less specific than electrical alternans.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diffuse ST-segment elevation is characteristic of acute pericarditis, not specifically cardiac tamponade. The ECG finding described is alternating QRS amplitude, not ST changes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While sinus tachycardia commonly occurs in tamponade, it reflects a constant elevated heart rate, not the beat-to-beat QRS amplitude alternation described in the question.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PR-segment depression is an ECG finding of acute pericarditis reflecting atrial inflammation. It does not cause beat-to-beat alternation in QRS amplitude.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis presents with increasing dyspnea and chest discomfort. Her blood pressure is 94/62 mm Hg with a 14 mm Hg drop in systolic pressure during inspiration. Heart rate is 112 beats/min. Neck veins are distended, and heart sounds are muffled. Echocardiography reveals a large pericardial effusion with diastolic collapse of the right atrium and right ventricle. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiation of emergent hemodialysis to correct the underlying uremia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urgent pericardiocentesis with needle aspiration of pericardial fluid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administration of intravenous furosemide to reduce fluid overload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone for presumed autoimmune pericarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Scheduling elective pericardiectomy within the next 48 to 72 hours

explanation.summary: This patient has cardiac tamponade with hemodynamic compromise (hypotension, pulsus paradoxus, JVD, muffled heart sounds, and echocardiographic evidence of right-sided chamber collapse). Emergent pericardiocentesis is the treatment of choice for cardiac tamponade.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cardiac tamponade with hemodynamic compromise, emergent drainage of the pericardial effusion is required. Pericardiocentesis, the percutaneous needle aspiration of pericardial fluid, is the treatment of choice. The removed fluid can also be sent for laboratory analysis to help identify the underlying cause.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While uremia is the likely underlying cause and hemodialysis will be needed, the immediate life-threatening problem is cardiac tamponade. Addressing the tamponade with pericardiocentesis takes priority over treating the underlying cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diuretics are inappropriate in cardiac tamponade. Reducing intravascular volume would further decrease preload and worsen hypotension in a patient already experiencing obstructive shock from tamponade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Corticosteroids do not address the acute hemodynamic compromise of cardiac tamponade. Even if autoimmune pericarditis were suspected, the immediate priority is drainage of the compressive effusion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elective pericardiectomy is not appropriate as an immediate intervention for hemodynamically unstable cardiac tamponade. The patient needs emergent pericardiocentesis now, not a scheduled surgical procedure days later.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of a recent ST-elevation myocardial infarction develops sudden onset hypotension, distended neck veins, and muffled heart sounds 5 days after the event. Echocardiography shows a moderate-sized pericardial effusion with right atrial diastolic collapse. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's pericardial effusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased plasma oncotic pressure from nephrotic syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myocardial rupture with hemorrhage into the pericardial space

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viral pericarditis causing increased capillary permeability in the pericardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypothyroidism-related increased capillary permeability and decreased lymphatic drainage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Malignant pericardial infiltration from metastatic disease

explanation.summary: Myocardial rupture is a feared mechanical complication of myocardial infarction, typically occurring 3-7 days post-MI. Rupture causes hemorrhage into the pericardial space (hemopericardium), which can rapidly lead to cardiac tamponade.
explanation.whyCorrect: Free wall myocardial rupture is a mechanical complication of MI that typically occurs 3-7 days after the event, when the infarcted myocardium is at its weakest during the inflammatory and granulation tissue phase. Blood hemorrhages into the pericardial space, causing hemopericardium and acute cardiac tamponade, as demonstrated by this patient's presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nephrotic syndrome causes pericardial effusion through decreased plasma oncotic pressure, but there is no indication of nephrotic syndrome in this patient. The temporal relationship to the recent MI strongly suggests a post-MI complication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Viral pericarditis could cause effusion but would typically present with pleuritic chest pain, friction rub, and diffuse ST changes. The acute onset 5 days post-MI with tamponade physiology is most consistent with myocardial rupture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypothyroidism can cause pericardial effusion but develops slowly and does not typically cause acute tamponade. The presentation immediately following MI makes this diagnosis unlikely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Malignant pericardial effusion develops gradually over weeks to months. The acute onset of tamponade 5 days after MI in an elderly patient is most consistent with mechanical rupture.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is found to have a small pericardial effusion incidentally on echocardiography performed for evaluation of a heart murmur. He is asymptomatic with normal vital signs and no jugular venous distention. The effusion measures approximately 1 cm in width circumferentially. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent pericardiocentesis to prevent progression to cardiac tamponade

choices.label: B
choices.text: Treatment of any identifiable underlying condition without mandatory drainage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate surgical creation of a pericardial window for continuous drainage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy for presumed bacterial pericarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Initiation of anticoagulation therapy to prevent pericardial clot formation

explanation.summary: In a hemodynamically stable patient with a small pericardial effusion and no evidence of cardiac tamponade, drainage is not necessary. Treatment of the underlying condition may lead to resolution of the effusion without the need for invasive intervention.
explanation.whyCorrect: If a patient with pericardial effusion is hemodynamically stable with no evidence of cardiac tamponade, it is not necessary to drain the effusion. Treatment of the underlying condition may lead to clinical improvement or resolution without drainage. Small, asymptomatic effusions found incidentally are often considered benign and may be idiopathic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Emergent pericardiocentesis is reserved for patients with cardiac tamponade and hemodynamic compromise. A small, incidental effusion in a stable patient does not warrant invasive drainage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A pericardial window is used for continuous drainage in cases of recurrent effusions after pericardiocentesis. It is not indicated for a small, asymptomatic, incidental effusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Empiric antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of bacterial infection. Small, asymptomatic effusions are often idiopathic or related to benign causes and do not require antibiotic therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulation could worsen a hemorrhagic pericardial effusion and is not indicated for management of a simple pericardial effusion. There is no role for anticoagulation in preventing pericardial clot formation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer presents with progressive dyspnea over 6 weeks. Chest x-ray shows an enlarged cardiac silhouette with a globular, water-bottle shape. Echocardiography confirms a large pericardial effusion without signs of right-sided chamber collapse. Her blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, and heart rate is 82 beats/min. Which of the following characteristics of this pericardial effusion best explains why cardiac tamponade has not yet developed despite the large volume of fluid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The effusion is composed primarily of transudative fluid with low protein content

choices.label: B
choices.text: The effusion is loculated and confined to the posterior pericardial space away from the ventricles

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient's elevated systemic blood pressure prevents right-sided chamber compression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Malignant effusions have a lower viscosity that prevents compression of cardiac chambers

choices.label: E
choices.text: The slow, chronic accumulation has allowed the pericardium to gradually stretch and accommodate the fluid

explanation.summary: The speed of fluid accumulation is the most important factor determining whether tamponade develops. Slow, chronic accumulation allows the pericardium to gradually stretch and accommodate larger volumes of fluid without significant pressure increase, whereas rapid accumulation (as in trauma) can cause tamponade with as little as 100 mL.
explanation.whyCorrect: The speed of fluid accumulation is much more important than total volume in determining whether tamponade develops. Slow, chronic fluid accumulation in the pericardium allows for gradual stretching of the pericardium to accommodate more fluid, making urgent symptoms less common. In contrast, rapid accumulation prevents pericardial accommodation, and as little as 100 mL can be lethal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The composition of the fluid (transudative vs exudative) does not determine whether tamponade develops. The rate of accumulation and the pericardium's ability to stretch are the critical factors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The question describes a circumferential effusion visible on echocardiography. Even if loculated, the lack of tamponade is best explained by the slow rate of accumulation allowing pericardial accommodation, not the location of the fluid.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Systemic blood pressure does not prevent right-sided chamber compression. Tamponade depends on intrapericardial pressure relative to intracardiac pressures, and the low-pressure right-sided chambers are always most vulnerable.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The viscosity of the effusion fluid does not determine whether cardiac chamber compression occurs. The critical factor is the rate at which fluid accumulates relative to the pericardium's ability to stretch.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with cardiac tamponade has pulsus paradoxus detected on blood pressure measurement. During examination, the cuff is inflated above systolic pressure and then slowly deflated. Korotkoff sounds are first heard intermittently at 130 mm Hg and become audible with every heartbeat at 116 mm Hg. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of pulsus paradoxus in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased intrathoracic pressure during expiration compresses the pulmonary veins, reducing left ventricular filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: The pericardial effusion shifts position with respiration, intermittently compressing the left main coronary artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased intrathoracic pressure during inspiration causes air trapping, reducing oxygen delivery to the myocardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sympathetic activation during inspiration causes transient coronary vasospasm, reducing myocardial contractility

choices.label: E
choices.text: The compressed right ventricle cannot expand during inspiration, forcing the interventricular septum leftward and reducing left ventricular stroke volume

explanation.summary: Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mm Hg drop in systolic BP during inspiration) occurs in cardiac tamponade because inspiration increases venous return to the right side, but the compressed right ventricle cannot expand outward due to the tense effusion. The interventricular septum is forced leftward, decreasing LV volume, stroke volume, and blood pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: During inspiration, decreased intrathoracic pressure increases venous return to the right heart. In tamponade, the right ventricle is unable to expand outward due to the surrounding fluid under pressure.
